sentences.text: Well, we were reasonably mixed with the youth groups.
And frankly, we had a hard time to communicate simply because of the language barriers, you know.
So the Portuguese group was myself and my brother Abraham, my cousin Nissim Benuiz, B-E-N-U-I-Z, and two other brothers, two sets of other brothers.
And-- and forgive me, I-- Garcia, excuse me.
One set of brothers was Garcia, G-A-R-C-I-A, but the other one I cannot remember.
So it was a very, very-- a handful of Portuguese natives that went to Eretz amongst the-- all the hundreds of immigrants.
And so we stuck together pretty much.
Certainly we would try to mingle, but we had the problem.
Some of the-- the people stayed in Portugal very, very short periods of time.
Others had stayed a little longer, so they knew a few words in Portuguese.
So we would take advantage of that and try to communicate with them.
The day passed aboard ship-- I believe it took us about four days from Lisbon to Haifa with that-- including that detour.
But basically, you eat and sleep.
There was lot of dancing.

The-- these groups would suddenly-- well, the-- the physiognomies had changed drastically when they are in-- when they were in Portugal, as opposed when they were on board ship, because of the simple reason they felt they are going to Eretz.
They are going to the-- to the-- to Palestine, to-- to-- to Israel ultimately.
And-- and-- and that-- that was the goal, the main goal, you know.
And the freedom and-- and-- and to be in-- in Eretz was, to them, and not just for the youth, adults as well, was a big difference.
And you could very, very definitely see that showing in their-- in the-- in their punim, in their faces, so.
As I understand, and I stand to be corrected as well, the World Zionist Organization had a lot to do with that, the American Joint Committee, the HIAS, the HIAS.
And I believe that these were the main organizations which help with the funds, which they collected from other Jewish people to save Jewish lives.
And they paid for the trips.
I must say that the food was excellent aboard the The conditions were pretty good.
Accommodations were pretty good.
The only thing that was not so good was the sea waves.
They were rough at times crossing the Mediterranean.
We arrived in Israel, and here was a shocker really, again, perhaps the largest shocker to-- to-- more to the adults than-- than-- and to some children as well.
Because the [?

Sochnut, ?] the-- the-- the Jewish Agency, naturally was waiting for us, as well as members of His Majesty's Government, the Palestine police, along with the Arab police, and along with Jewish police.
They packed us into buses.
They greeted us with halvah, believe it or not.
I ate so much halvah that I was sick for months because it was so good.
You know, it's-- it's-- it's something-- I do not know exactly.
It's made out of almonds and-- and lots of sugar, and it's-- it's like a candy bar.
But it's so good that you just want to eat more and more and more.
And we were not used to it.
It was the first time we came across halvah.
So we became sick, and not just me, most of our kids get-- got sick.
But anyway, we were taken to about 20-- 20, 25 minutes ride to a camp, Athlit.
And this was a fenced, barbed-wire fenced, camp.
And here is where some of the refugees were really shocked, because they could not understand.
Why?
We just came-- we thought we were free, and now we are being put into these camps.
But basically, the stay was a-- a week, a few days, to some.
It was like a-- a transit camp, if you wish.
They would check-- check you out, check your papers.

Because at that particular point in time, 1944, I believe that there was-- the British government was concerned that the German fifth column would infiltrate the ships with the Jewish refugees and somehow come into Palestine and then go on from there to other places, I suppose.
So therefore, we were kept in those camps for-- for that period of time.
But once cleared, we were brought to-- the men were separated from the women.
And we were separated and then brought in to Haifa to Bat-Gailim, B-A-T--G-A-I-L-I-M, Bat-Gailim.
The

And here was a little bit disappointed-- disappointing, because the quarters were very, very small, and men, women, and children were all kept in the same-- the same buildings, and we were given basic mattresses.
But this was a temporary quarters anyway.
You were free.
That was the first time when you get to Eretz that you felt that you are free was Bat-Gailim.
Because you could do whatever you wanted to do.
You can go.
You can come.
And you can sleep.
You can do anything you want.
So-- and from there, of course, then we were taken to the [HEBREW] and there to the kibbutz, which I mentioned a little earlier, so.
Yes.
My schedule-- my schedule was to join-- to join the British Army.
And so--
No, no, no, no.
There was-- you were not forced to do that, obviously.
I wanted to do it.
And I went to the British Joint Office in Haifa, and I was asked by the-- by a lieutenant, a young lieutenant, British lieutenant, for my-- my papers.
So I showed him my legal papers, entry to Palestine, and I gave him my Portuguese passport.
So he looked at it.
He went back into another room, came back a few moments later.
He said, I'm sorry, sir, you can't join the British Army.
Portugal is a neutral country.
And that was the end of it.
So then I joined the PPRD, which is the Palestine Police Railroad Division, in Haifa, as a-- as a guard, railroad guard, if you wish.
We had good quarters.
We had good uniforms, good food.
And I didn't know anybody else in-- in Palestine, so I had a home, so to speak.
But I stayed there for about six months, and then I went to-- to Nahariyyah, because I had been requested for this [NON-ENGLISH] family ultimately.
And I stayed there for a short period of time, and then I joined the JSP, the Jewish Settlement Police.
The Jewish Settlement Police, interesting enough, was a cover-up for the Haganah, for the Israeli underground.
So we used to do our duties while the British officers would come in from Nazareth or Tiberius.
And after that, we used to switch and then do the Haganah training and so forth.
No, not really.
We did not.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was-- there was something that's interesting.
When I was with the-- I served for a short period of time with the 2nd Battalion of the Jewish Brigade Group.
My commanding officer was Haim L-A-S-K-O-W, L-A-S-K-O-W, who later on became the Chief of Staff of the Israeli Army.
And the-- my drill sergeant was Sergeant Tal.
Later on, he also became a general of the Israel Defense Forces.
But we used to do some very, very, very basic-- basic training, like you do in any army.
Ultimately, it paid off because we were able to use that training for the War of Independence, so.
Oh, yes.
Oh, absolutely, yeah, yes, yeah.
And-- but when I joined the British Army, the-- the second time around, I left the Jewish-- the-- the-- the Settlement Police, and I went to-- near Tel Aviv there is a camp by the name of Sarafand.
It was a very large camp.
So I came in all prepared.
I had promised myself, this time I'm going to be a liar.
So when I came to the-- to the Joint office, I was asked for my papers.
So I said, I'm sorry, I don't have any papers.
How did you come to Palestine?
I said, I came legally.
Where did you come from?
I'm a native of Bulgaria, Sofia.
But I came to Palestine via Portugal, because our family had ended up in Portugal.
We had lost all our papers, and that's how come.
And there was a Jewish officer in the room when I was being interviewed by the British.
And the guy looked at me, he looked at me, and then when we went out into another hall, he said in Hebrew, [HEBREW].
He said, you are-- you are Bulgarian like I'm a Chinese, but what do I care?
And that's how come I joined the British Army as a Bulgarian, native of Sofia.
Because I knew one thing, that the British, they don't speak Bulgarian anymore I didn't, right?
So in my British Army papers, I am a Bulgarian.
And we went to-- we were taken from Sarafand to Ismailia in Egypt.
I can't remember names of officers or anything.
I just get-- we had British drill sergeants, Irish drill sergeants, Scottish, Israelis.
And we spent some time in the ITD, the so-called Infantry Training Depot.
And we had accelerated training.
All the Jewish Brigade was trained very acceleratedly so we could be going to war.
But basically, the end of the war came at that point in time when we were in training in Egypt.
So ultimately, we went to-- we ended up in Europe with BAOR, the so-called British Army On the Rhine, was the abbreviation of it, but with the Jewish Brigade.
And I was then switched to the corps of military police.
I served with the 79th Unit of the British Military Police, within the Jewish Brigade Group.
And if I may say, one of the most touchy experiences that I ever had with refugees of the Holocaust was with the dispersed persons.
Because I was on duty with another fellow next to the main train station in Antwerpen, and that main train station, the central, was bringing the trains from Germany directly to Antwerpen.
By the way, in Brussels was the center of the black market in Europe at that point in time.
And we always used to watch who was doing what, you know.
But nevertheless, I was standing there, you know, at attention pretty much, like the military police stay, you know?
And a group of Jewish dispersed persons came out of the train.
Now, by serving with the Jewish Brigade Group, we had the Magen David on the-- on the arm.
[CRYING] I'm sorry.
And they kissed our arm.
[CRYING] They cried.
They kissed our arm.
One said in Yiddish, [YIDDISH] [CRYING] They were very happy to see us.
It was very hard.
[CRYING] I'm sorry.
That was one of the hardest moments that I experienced, even though I was not there as a survivor of the Holocaust.
But these were our brothers, our sisters that made it, so.
Yes, we did.
We went back to-- to Egypt actually, and we were discharged back in Sarafand, 1940-- 1946.
And then I went back to Nahariyyah, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I worked for a fellow by the name of Willie [NON-ENGLISH].
Nahariyyah-- Haifa we used to bring in the supplies to Nahariyyah.
And-- but shortly thereafter, we got involved in-- of course, we were-- we were members of the Haganah.
Most of everybody that came from the army-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] excuse me-- went into the Haganah.
And we had training all the time, variety of trainings, and different arms, ammunitions.
We had obviously the largest arsenal in the world in as much as variety is concerned, nothing as much as quantity is concerned.
We had a machine gun from Poland, the-- the Polish [? wrekm. ?] Or we had a-- a Tommy gun from Finland, it was Finnish.
We had handguns from Spain or rifles from Canada, carbines from Italy.
And it was-- it was amazing.
And I served for a while in the Haganah.
I was-- me and our members of our group, we were asked one time just before the war started, or the action started, we felt that we have to have our arms, and our arms were hidden.
I don't know how you say it in English, [HEBREW].
In Hebrew, it's called [HEBREW].
It's a hidden-- hidden place for the arms.
And we had one place near the Haifa-Beirut highway, just outside.
It was a potato field.
And what the-- the-- [HEBREW], the guy who was in charge of the arms, he had buried these arms into a pipe, within probably a 2-feet-diameter water pipe, and sealed quite well in the potato field.
So we had received the order, OK, now we have to get the arms out.
We had two [HEBREW], two hidden places.
One was the potato field.
The other one was the beach in Nahariyyah.
But by the time the British came into the area, they build a-- an RR camp right next-- next-- close-- very close to the-- where one of the [HEBREW] was located.

That was difficult to do, because we had to go in and dig by hand in the sand, and one guy at a time, before we could pull out the whole thing, and the fear of being shot by or being caught by the British.
But we managed to take the arms out, and-- and we used to train.
We used-- we used to walk four and a half hours from Nahariyyah to kibbutz [HEBREW].
And-- and to see-- we used to bypass the-- some of the Arab villages on the way, just to see what would happen if-- well, when the war comes in, you know.
And we were able to work most of the time unnoticed.
We were almost caught by the British on different occasions because there was a curfew from midnight till 6 o'clock in the morning.
No one could be on the streets or on the highways.
And we used to train, and we were caught once by a British patrol.
And fortunately enough, we were-- we were planning-- we already had our answers ahead of time.

So when we-- we heard the-- that-- that command, halt, who is there or who goes there, then our lieutenant [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], later on became [NON-ENGLISH] Gal, G-A-L, he-- he answered the-- the British and told him, of course, that we were a-- a team of boxers training for an exhibition coming out.
And we start to play boxing and all that, running, and doing like this.
And they said, OK, and then they let us go.
But there was always some danger involved into that.
And certainly during these trainings, we also provided escorts to convoys between Haifa and the Kiryat Motzkin-- Nahariyyah-Kiryat Motzkin, Kiryat Motzkin-Nahariyyah, you know.
And in each-- in each one of the vehicles-- it was a bus, a truck, a car, a motorcycle-- there was always someone from the Haganah, in most cases unarmed.
Only the [HEBREW], the [HEBREW], the lieutenant, would have some kind of a small gun, small arm, to help in case of need-- attack by the Arabs and so forth, which had happened on many occasions.

Several of the Jewish people from Nahariyyah that I knew were caught in-- in the city of Akko, and they were killed just before the action starts, because there was already tension mounting, you know.
Ultimately, the Arabs, they closed the city of Akko, so you couldn't even go home.
We had to use a boat from Kiryat Motzkin to Nahariyyah by-- by-- by the sea because of the Arab blockade.
But the Haganah didn't give up.
And we were able to maneuver the blockade, bringing food by ships.
And no, it was not easy, but we made it.
We did it.
Oh--
--very much so, very much so.

The-- the-- there were thousands, thousands of dispersed persons that came into Eretz in Palestine, to Palestine illegally, simply because the British government, they had that so-called paper that they did not want any more Jews in-- in-- in Palestine.

At one time there was-- there was some statistics made by the British government that they stated that's not-- there's not enough nails to build-- to build a home for one more set of refugees, something like that.
The reason they did it was simply to appease the-- to-- to please the Arabs and not-- not to please the Jews.
And-- because the Jews had nowhere to go.
The dispersed persons, absolutely no one-- nobody wanted them.
And--
Well, please understand that my responsibility was the same as everybody else's responsibility.
We all had the same responsibility.
And in this case, dealing directly with the dispersed persons, you-- you had to deal with the illegal immigration, which was the so-called Aliyah Bet.

And what it-- what it really was was the immigrants, they were put on board ships, in many cases, and perhaps in most cases, in unseaworthy ships that would be bought from dealers, I don't know, Italy and Greece, France, or wherever it was, wherever they could.

The-- the-- the-- the Jewish Committee-- the-- the-- I can't even remember exactly, to the Haganah that-- and to the agents throughout Europe, wherever they were able to buy a ship, they would buy a ship, however small it was or however big it was.
But most of the problems, and perhaps the main problem, was not just the ship itself, because business is business.
Anybody will sell, and anybody would buy that had the money.
To buy a ship was much easier.
But to conceal-- to conceal the purchasing of that ship so that the British intelligence that was always in-- working in order to find out, where are the Jews loading the next ship for Palestine.
Because they had the British Navy on their side, the British intelligence.

They had the British Air Force working for them to-- to patrol the seas, the coast of Europe to find out if they can pinpoint any illegal ship and then advise the navy to go and-- and-- and stop that ship.
And stop they did.
There were many, many ships that, however unseaworthy they were, they were sent back to the high seas.
When they were close to Palestine, and when they-- when they could see the shores of Eretz Israel, they were not allowed to disembark.
You know, many thousands of people died because of it.

If there's anything that-- that really aggravates me about the British government, the policies, was to ship-- to send the ships back to the high seas when they really had no right to do it, absolutely no right, no human right.
That was inhumane.
But our function in the Haganah was to help when we were notified.

For instance, in Nahariyyah, or the Nahariyyah Haganah command of the Western Galilee, would be notified, well, we are expecting a ship, for instance, the with, I don't know, 1,600 people, some-- something like that, and they are due on this date.
But they-- we never knew when, because they used to try to outmaneuver the British Navy, the British Air Force, and they would go from place to place to place, et cetera.
But the moment the ship would arrive by the-- by the shores, then there were a multitude of young men, Haganah young men, we used to swim with ropes to-- close to the sea and have these heavy ropes.
And then what happens in [? there, ?] and it's hard to understand, is the fact that many people that couldn't even swim, the moment they saw the shores they would jump out of the boat.
And many times you couldn't even stand.
The-- the water was above your head.
And therefore, the Haganah, the young men, were there to help these people and guide them through the ropes into the shores.
Into the shores, put it in a bus, in a car, et cetera, and haul them away to freedom.
Undescribable.
You cannot describe that.
Because the-- just to be free in the land of Israel, it was the ultimate.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it's hard to describe the-- the-- the tears that-- that-- that you would see in their faces, the happy-- tears of happiness in this case.
